confirmed.title: Hourly "next 48 h" chart is skewed by the active location's UTC offset (Date.now() compared against location-local times)
confirmed.evidence: `const hourly = nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48);` calls `nextHours` with its default `now = Date.now()` (hourly.ts:54). Inside `nextHours`, each point's time is read via `localEpoch(point.time)` = `Date.UTC(...)` of the location-local wall-clock string (Open-Meteo timezone=auto, no zone suffix), then kept when `localEpoch >= now`. But `Date.now()` is the true UTC instant, whereas the point times are the location's LOCAL time read AS UTC, so the two are off by the location's `utc_offset_seconds`. This is the exact mirror of the FR-ANIM-02 bug the team already fixed in `isDaytime` (which deliberately shifts `Date.now()` by `weather.utcOffsetSeconds`; see lib/animated-bg/day-night.ts nowTimeOfDay + WeatherBackground.tsx:197) — but that shift is NOT applied here. I reproduced it: for Kyiv (offset +3h) at local 14:00 the chart's first plotted hour is 11:00 (3 already-elapsed hours shown, and it stops ~3 h early); for a UTC-7 city at local 14:00 it starts at 21:00, dropping the first 7 genuinely-future hours. `forecast.utcOffsetSeconds` is available at this exact call site (it is read two lines of logic later for the background publish). No test catches it: hourly.test.ts always injects an absolute `now`, and the integration test never drives `nextHours` with the +10800s offset it asserts on the forecast.
confirmed.suggestion: Pass a location-aware `now` so the slice boundary is in the location's frame, matching the isDaytime fix. E.g. `const offsetMs = (forecast.utcOffsetSeconds ?? 0) * 1000; const hourly = nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48, Date.now() + offsetMs);` (since point times are read as UTC-of-local, shifting `now` by the offset realigns them). Add a unit/integration case that asserts the first plotted hour equals the location-local current hour for a non-zero offset (e.g. +10800).
verifierNotes:
CONFIRMED — the mechanism is real and reproduces exactly as claimed. End-to-end verification:

1. Call site: components/forecast/ForecastSection.tsx:276 is literally `const hourly = nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48);` — no `now` arg, so it falls back to the default.

2. lib/forecast/hourly.ts:51-65: `nextHours(hourly, count = 48, now = Date.now())`. It keeps a point when `localEpoch(point.time) >= now`, where `localEpoch` (lines 31-44) = `Date.UTC(...)` of the location-local wall-clock string. `Date.now()` is the TRUE UTC instant; the point times are the location's LOCAL time read AS UTC. The two operands differ by the location's `utc_offset_seconds`. The skew direction/magnitude is exactly the location offset.

3. timezone=auto confirmed (app/api/forecast/route.ts:105, asserted in route.test.ts:170 and the integration test:183) — so hourly `time` strings are location-local with no zone suffix, exactly the precondition the bug needs. types.ts:16 documents this.

4. The identical bug WAS fixed for day/night but the fix is NOT applied here: isDaytime (lib/animated-bg/day-night.ts:73-94) shifts the absolute instant by `utcOffsetSeconds`; WeatherBackground.tsx:197 passes `weather.utcOffsetSeconds`; there is a dedicated regression test (components/shell/WeatherBackground.timezone.test.tsx) whose header states the review gate found the prior isDaytime "reduced the absolute now... THEIR clock." The same offset is available at the nextHours call site — it is read 36 lines later at ForecastSection.tsx:211-214 (`forecast.utcOffsetSeconds`) for the background publish — but never threaded into nextHours.

5. No test catches it: lib/forecast/hourly.test.ts always injects an absolute `now` minted on the SAME Date.UTC basis as the series (internally consistent, never exercises an offset). components/forecast/ForecastSection.test.tsx only asserts the chart receives data-points > 0 (line 218), never the first plotted hour vs a non-zero offset. tests/integration/weekend-trip-flow.integration.test.ts asserts `fc.utcOffsetSeconds === 10_800` on the SERVICE-layer Forecast (line 173) and its setSystemTime timezone blocks (lines 253-289) drive only the weekend-date selection (selectWeekend/upcomingWeekend), never nextHours or the chart.

Reproduction (node, same logic as hourly.ts) matched the finding's numbers precisely: Kyiv +3h at local 14:00 → buggy first plotted hour 11:00 (37 pts, ~3h elapsed shown); UTC-7 at local 14:00 → buggy first plotted hour 21:00 (27 pts, first 7 future hours dropped); the suggested `Date.now() + offsetMs` fix realigns both to 14:00.

Every refutation angle fails: correct file, not guarded at the call site, reachable main render path once a forecast loads, framework does not handle it, and it IS applicable to this keyless static app (the data is location-local precisely because of timezone=auto). Severity kept at critical: it is a UX/correctness defect (mis-windowed/mislabeled "next 48 h" chart, leading hours dropped or already-elapsed hours shown) rather than data corruption or a crash, but the team already treated the identical isDaytime bug as critical-worthy (review-gate fix + dedicated regression test), the project bar is explicitly "honest under failure" with evals ≥ 90, and FR-FORECAST-03 is directly violated — so keep is the consistent call.

CONFIRMED end-to-end; not refuted. File/line correct: components/forecast/ForecastSection.tsx:276 calls `nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48)` with no `now`, so it defaults to `Date.now()` (the true UTC instant) per lib/forecast/hourly.ts:54. Inside nextHours, each point's time is read via localEpoch = Date.UTC(...) of a zone-less wall-clock string (hourly.ts:31-44). The route requests timezone=auto (app/api/forecast/route.ts:105), so Open-Meteo emits location-LOCAL ISO strings (no zone suffix) plus a top-level utc_offset_seconds that is parsed into forecast.utcOffsetSeconds (lib/forecast/validation.ts:130-138). Thus the slice boundary (true UTC) and the point times (local-read-as-UTC) differ by the location's offset — exactly as claimed.

User-visible: HourlyChart.tsx renders the hour on the XAxis (tickFormatter=hourTick) and tooltip label, so the wrong hours are plotted visibly.

Reproduced the finding's exact numbers with a standalone Node script: Kyiv (+3h) at local 14:00 -> chart first plotted hour = 11:00 (three already-elapsed/past hours shown); a UTC-7 city at local 14:00 -> first plotted hour = 21:00 (first 7 genuinely-future hours dropped). The suggested fix (now + offsetMs) realigns both to 14:00.

The 'mirror bug already fixed elsewhere' claim is accurate: WeatherBackground.tsx:197 passes weather.utcOffsetSeconds to isDaytime, which deliberately shifts now into the location frame (lib/animated-bg/day-night.ts:80-87). The equivalent shift is simply absent at the nextHours call site — a genuine, demonstrable inconsistency on the same data.

No test catches it: lib/forecast/hourly.test.ts always injects an absolute now; tests/integration/weekend-trip-flow.integration.test.ts asserts fc.utcOffsetSeconds === 10800 (line 173) but never drives nextHours against a real clock (its setSystemTime calls at 254-302 exercise the day/night background, not the chart); ForecastSection.test.tsx mocks the chart and only asserts the series is non-empty (line 217).

Reachability for a real keyless no-DB user: high. Kyiv (UTC+2/+3) is the app's canonical Ukrainian-first location; the bug triggers for essentially any non-UTC location on a normal session right after the core 'select a location' action — no edge case, no URL tampering, no special steps. This is a real defect for a real user.

One refinement that does NOT change the verdict: with forecast_days=7, Open-Meteo returns ~168 hourly points, so the 48-slot slice almost always FILLS 48. The dominant effect is therefore a window SHIFT (past hours included at the start, an equal number of future hours omitted, window ends early) rather than truncation to fewer than 48; only a near-empty series would actually shorten the count. The finding's 'stops ~3h early / drops the first 7 future hours' correctly describes the shifted window. Keep severity critical/high: the 'next 48 h' chart (FR-FORECAST-03) mislabels already-elapsed hours as upcoming on the default location every session, and it is the direct mirror of a bug the team already chose to fix in isDaytime. Suggested fix is correct: const offsetMs = (forecast.utcOffsetSeconds ?? 0) * 1000; nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48, Date.now() + offsetMs), with an added test asserting the first plotted hour equals the location-local current hour for a non-zero offset (e.g. +10800).

confirmed.title: CSP allows 'unsafe-inline' (and no nonce/hash) for script-src — XSS mitigation is weaker than the rest of the posture
confirmed.evidence: The Content-Security-Policy sets `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'`. Because no untrusted HTML is ever injected (no dangerouslySetInnerHTML/innerHTML/eval anywhere in the tree, all upstream payloads are zod-parsed and rendered as text), there is no concrete reflected/stored XSS code path today, so this is not release-blocking. But `'unsafe-inline'` removes CSP's defense-in-depth value: if any future slice introduces an injection sink, an attacker-supplied inline `<script>` would execute. The file itself documents this as a production-hardening follow-up. connect-src/img-src/default-src are correctly locked to 'self' (+ OSM tiles), so egress is well constrained.
confirmed.suggestion: Track tightening script-src (and style-src) to nonces or hashes as a post-MVP hardening item. Next 16 supports a nonce-based CSP via middleware; Leaflet's inline styles can be covered by a style hash or a scoped stylesheet. Not a release blocker for this keyless, auth-free, secret-free app, but it is the one place the header posture is softer than the rest of the design.
verifierNotes: Confirmed end-to-end against the code. (1) Cited location is exact: next.config.ts:42 is `"script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'"`, joined into the Content-Security-Policy applied to every route via headers() (source `/:path*`). (2) The finding's stated premise — no concrete XSS sink today — is true: a repo-wide grep over app/, lib/, components/ for dangerouslySetInnerHTML|innerHTML|outerHTML|insertAdjacentHTML|document.write|eval(|new Function|string-arg setTimeout/setInterval found only one hit, components/shell/empty-state.test.tsx:55, which is a test-only READ of container.innerHTML for a regex assertion, not a write/injection sink. (3) The CSP is the only one in effect and is genuinely un-tightened: there is no middleware.ts, no <meta http-equiv> CSP, and no per-route header override anywhere (grep confirmed), so `'unsafe-inline'` is not nonced/hashed elsewhere and Next does not auto-inject a nonce here. The described mechanism (CSP would not block an attacker-supplied inline <script> should a sink ever be added) is therefore accurate, and the asymmetry it names — default-src/connect-src/img-src locked to 'self' (+OSM tiles) while script-src/style-src carry 'unsafe-inline' — is real. The finding correctly scopes this as defense-in-depth / post-MVP hardening with no present exploit path, and the file's own comment (lines 33-36) documents the same follow-up. Severity minor is appropriate and not overstated; keep.

Verified at next.config.ts:42 — the CSP literally sets `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'` (and style-src likewise). The finding's supporting claims all check out: grep across app/components/lib found NO dangerouslySetInnerHTML, NO runtime innerHTML writes (the only innerHTML hit is a read assertion in components/shell/empty-state.test.tsx:55), NO eval(, NO new Function. No URL/attribute injection vector exists either: the only href values are hardcoded constants (OPEN_METEO_URL/OSM_URL in AppFooter.tsx), searchParams are consumed server-side to build upstream Open-Meteo URLs (app/api/forecast/route.ts) rather than reflected into DOM, and the only setAttribute writes a fixed data-theme token (ThemeProvider.tsx). connect-src/img-src/default-src are correctly locked to 'self' (+ OSM tiles) as claimed.

The finding does NOT assert a present, exploitable XSS — it explicitly states there is no concrete reflected/stored XSS path today, marks it not release-blocking, rates it minor, and frames it solely as lost defense-in-depth should a future slice add an injection sink. That scoping is accurate and not overstated. The file itself documents tightening 'unsafe-inline' to nonces/hashes as a production-hardening follow-up, corroborating the suggestion.

The strongest refutation candidate — that 'unsafe-inline' is currently required for Next 16's inline hydration bootstrap and, with zero sinks, residual risk today is nil — is already conceded by the finding and does not contradict its actual claim (that the header posture is softer here than elsewhere and should be tracked post-MVP). No wrong file/line/mechanism, no guarding test, no framework behavior that nullifies it. The claim is true as written; severity 'minor' is appropriate. Cannot refute.

confirmed.title: Hourly 48h chart window is offset by the active location's UTC offset (viewer-clock vs location-local frame mismatch)
confirmed.evidence: Forecast spec 'Render a 48-hour hourly temperature line chart': the chart SHALL cover 'the next 48 hours' from now. `nextHours(hourly, count=48, now=Date.now())` keeps points where `localEpoch(point.time) >= now`. But `localEpoch` parses the timezone=auto ISO-LOCAL string via `Date.UTC(y,m-1,d,h)` (treating the location's wall-clock as UTC), while the production caller `ForecastSection.tsx:276` passes the DEFAULT `now = Date.now()` (a true absolute UTC instant) — two different frames. Reproduced for a Kyiv (+3h) location at the true instant 09:00Z (= 12:00 local): nextHours starts the window at the 09:00 LOCAL hour (3 hours stale) instead of 12:00 local; with the offset correction it correctly starts at 12:00. So the 'next 48 h' window is shifted by the location's UTC offset (eastern offsets include already-past local hours; western offsets drop near-future hours). The unit test (lib/forecast/hourly.test.ts:51) injects `now` on the SAME Date.UTC basis as the series, masking the production Date.now() path; the integration test never renders the chart. Notably, the sibling day/night path WAS fixed to thread `utcOffsetSeconds` (current-state.md:97 'the review-gate fixed a prior viewer-clock bug here'), but the identical fix was not applied to nextHours, even though `forecast.utcOffsetSeconds` is available at the call site.
confirmed.suggestion: Thread the location's offset into the slice: add a `utcOffsetSeconds` parameter to `nextHours` and compare `localEpoch(point.time) >= now + utcOffsetSeconds*1000` (mirroring `nowTimeOfDay` in lib/animated-bg/day-night.ts), then update the caller to `nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48, Date.now(), forecast.utcOffsetSeconds)`. Add a unit test that passes a true-UTC `now` plus a non-zero offset to lock the corrected frame.
verifierNotes:
CONFIRMED — not refuted. The claimed mechanism is real and reachable end-to-end in production.

Verified chain:
1. lib/forecast/hourly.ts:31-44 `localEpoch` parses Open-Meteo `timezone=auto` ISO-local strings (no zone suffix) via `Date.UTC(...)`, i.e. it treats the LOCATION's wall clock as if it were UTC.
2. hourly.ts:51-65 `nextHours(hourly, count=48, now=Date.now())` keeps points where `localEpoch(point.time) >= now`. Default `now` is `Date.now()` — a true absolute UTC instant.
3. Production caller components/forecast/ForecastSection.tsx:276 calls `nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48)` with NO `now` and NO offset → the default `Date.now()` path is taken, so the comparison mixes the location-local-as-UTC frame (epoch) with the true-UTC frame (now). They differ by exactly the location's UTC offset.
4. app/api/forecast/route.ts:105 sets `timezone=auto`, so the hourly `time` strings are the location's local wall clock and `utc_offset_seconds` is that location's offset. lib/forecast/validation.ts:103-104 `zipHourly` passes the `time` strings through verbatim (no UTC normalization), confirming they reach `nextHours` as local wall clock.
5. The sliced result feeds directly into the visible chart (ForecastSection.tsx:299 → HourlyChart.tsx), whose X-axis labels the local hour (hourTick), so the shift is user-visible (wrong leftmost hour).

Numerical reproduction (Kyiv +3, true instant 09:00Z = 12:00 local): buggy window starts at the 09:00 LOCAL hour (3h stale, leaks already-past hours); the offset-corrected comparison (`epoch >= now + utcOffsetSeconds*1000`, mirroring day-night.ts:84) correctly starts at 12:00 local. Eastern (positive) offsets leak past hours; western (negative) offsets drop near-future hours. Violates the forecast spec's "next 48 hours from now".

Asymmetry / sibling-fix claim verified: lib/animated-bg/day-night.ts threads `utcOffsetSeconds` (nowTimeOfDay shifts `now` by the offset) and IS wired in production at components/shell/WeatherBackground.tsx:197 (`isDaytime(now, ..., weather.utcOffsetSeconds)`); docs/current-state.md:97 records "the review-gate fixed a prior viewer-clock bug here." The identical fix was NOT applied to `nextHours`, even though `forecast.utcOffsetSeconds` is available at the same call site (read at ForecastSection.tsx:211-214 for the background publish).

Test-masking claim verified: lib/forecast/hourly.test.ts builds the series from BASE=`Date.UTC(...)` (line 31) and injects `now` via `nowAtHour()` (line 51-53) on the SAME `Date.UTC` basis, so series and `now` share one frame — exactly the artificial alignment the production `Date.now()` path breaks. No test passes a true-UTC `now` together with a non-zero offset; the integration/route tests never render the chart.

Refutation attempts all fail: not a wrong file; not guarded elsewhere (the offset is never threaded into nextHours, and the value isn't ~0 since the active location's offset is intrinsic to the explored city, independent of the viewer); the path is the default/only production call; no framework handling; it is a real user-facing correctness/spec defect in this keyless static app, not masked by degradation (the chart renders, just for the wrong hours).

Suggested fix is correct (sign verified): add `utcOffsetSeconds` param and compare `localEpoch(point.time) >= now + utcOffsetSeconds*1000`, caller `nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48, Date.now(), forecast.utcOffsetSeconds)`.

Severity: keep "major". It is an always-on spec violation ("next 48h from now") in a core user-facing chart for every non-UTC-aligned location (i.e. the app's whole "explore another city" premise), bounded by the offset (≤~14h) and non-crashing — major is well-calibrated, neither inflated to critical nor reducible to minor/cosmetic.

CONFIRMED end-to-end; reproduced numerically. Mechanism verified across the full chain:

1. Route requests timezone=auto (app/api/forecast/route.ts:105), so Open-Meteo returns hourly `time` as ISO-LOCAL (location wall-clock, no zone suffix). zipHourly passes them through verbatim (lib/forecast/validation.ts:103-104), so forecast.hourly[].time is genuinely location-local.
2. lib/forecast/hourly.ts:31-44 `localEpoch` parses those strings via Date.UTC(...) — treating the location's wall-clock as UTC.
3. lib/forecast/hourly.ts:54 default `now = Date.now()`; the production caller components/forecast/ForecastSection.tsx:276 calls `nextHours(forecast.hourly, 48)` with only 2 args, so `now` is a TRUE absolute-UTC instant. The two operands of `epoch >= now` (hourly.ts:62) are therefore in different frames, shifted by the location's UTC offset.
4. forecast.utcOffsetSeconds IS available at that exact call site (Forecast type carries it, types.ts:72; the same component reads it 65 lines earlier at line 211-214 for the background publish) yet is NOT threaded into nextHours.
5. The sibling day/night path WAS fixed to thread the offset: components/shell/WeatherBackground.tsx:197 passes weather.utcOffsetSeconds as isDaytime's 4th arg; docs/current-state.md documents 'the review-gate fixed a prior viewer-clock bug here.' The identical fix was not applied to nextHours — asymmetry is real.

Numeric reproduction (ran a faithful copy of localEpoch/nextHours): Kyiv +3h, true instant 09:00Z (= 12:00 local) → buggy window starts at the 09:00 LOCAL hour (3h stale, already-past hours leak in); with `now + offset*1000` it correctly starts at 12:00. Western cross-check (UTC-7, true 20:00Z = 13:00 local) → buggy window starts at 20:00 local, dropping near-future hours 13:00-19:00. The shift magnitude equals the full offset (3h for all Ukrainian cities), far exceeding the 1h point granularity, so it is plainly user-visible.

Test masking confirmed: the unit test injects `now` via nowAtHour() on the SAME Date.UTC(BASE...) basis as the series (lib/forecast/hourly.test.ts:31,51-53), so it never exercises the production Date.now() frame. The integration test (components/forecast/ForecastSection.test.tsx) mocks the chart to expose only data-points COUNT (lines 53-54,62-63) and its sole chart assertion checks count>0 (lines 212-219) — it never inspects the window, never mocks Date.now(), and makeForecast() (lines 80-101) omits utcOffsetSeconds. Neither test can observe the bug.

Refutation attempts all failed: not the wrong file (the headline 'file' lib/forecast/hourly.ts is imprecise — the actual defect is the caller — but the evidence explicitly and correctly cites ForecastSection.tsx:276); not guarded elsewhere (only the sibling was fixed); fully reachable (it is the live render path for every non-UTC location, i.e. every Ukrainian city); keyless/no-DB is irrelevant since this is pure client-side frame arithmetic; not covered by any passing test. Severity kept at major: it is a genuine, user-facing spec-compliance defect against 'the next 48 hours from now,' but a window-shift/correctness issue (past hours leak in, near-future hours can drop) rather than a crash or data corruption — consistent with major, not critical. The suggested fix (thread utcOffsetSeconds into nextHours and compare against now + utcOffsetSeconds*1000, mirroring nowTimeOfDay in lib/animated-bg/day-night.ts) is correct and aligns with the existing back-compat pattern.

contested.title: ThemeProvider seeds theme from prefers-color-scheme in a useState initializer → hydration mismatch + console error on dark-OS devices
